results.possibleIdentity: David F. Bantz is an engineer and computer scientist who was highly active in the fields of computer graphics, computer science, and systems engineering beginning in the late 1960s. He earned several degrees from Columbia University, including an A.B. in physics and a B.Sc. in electrical engineering in 1965, an M.Sc. in 1967, and an Eng. Sc.D. in electrical engineering and computer science in 1970. Following a brief period at a startup, he joined IBM's T. J. Watson Research Center in 1972, where he spent much of his career as a Research Staff Member until 2005. His work at IBM and Columbia (where he served as an Adjunct Professor for nearly 25 years) spanned numerous technical domains, including early computer graphics, wireless LAN design, and autonomic computing. He was also a key figure in the development of wearable computing, co-authoring papers on projects such as the IBM/Columbia Student Electronic Notebook in the early 1990s. Later in his career, he served as a professor in the Computer Science Department at the University of Southern Maine. His historical association with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) is consistent with his background as a technical expert in New York City during the organization's peak years, where he was recorded in archival directories as an engineer interested in collaborating with artists.
